title: Bistable Quad-Nets Composed of Four-Bar Linkages
abstract: 我们研究由空间四杆机构组成的机械结构，这些机构具有双稳态特性，即允许存在两种不同的构型。这些结构可解释为Study二次曲面上的四边形网格，这一特性可用于证明此类任意大规模结构的存在性。我们提出了一种纯几何构造方法，从欧几里得空间中的无穷小柔性四边形网格出发，应用Whiteley去平均化技术。该视角将问题置于离散微分几何的更广泛框架内，并使得能够从已知的四边形网格类别（如离散极小曲面）构造双稳态结构。所提出的构造方法不依赖于数值优化，并允许对轴位置和突跳角度进行控制。

Bistable Quad-Nets Composed of Four-Bar Linkages
Gudrun Szewieczek
gudrun.szewieczek@uibk.ac.at
Daniel Huczala
daniel.huczala@snu.ac.kr
Martin Pfurner
martin.pfurner@uibk.ac.at
Hans-Peter Schröcker
hans-peter.schroecker@uibk.ac.at
University of Innsbruck, Department of Basic Sciences in Engineering Sciences, Innsbruck, Austria
Seoul National University, Robotics Laboratory, Seoul, Korea
Abstract
We study mechanical structures composed of spatial four-bar linkages that are bistable, that is, they allow for two distinct configurations. They have an interpretation as quad nets in the Study quadric which can be used to prove existence of arbitrarily large structures of this type. We propose a purely geometric construction of such examples, starting from infinitesimally flexible quad nets in Euclidean space and applying Whiteley de-averaging. This point of view situates the problem within the broader framework of discrete differential geometry and enables the construction of bistable structures from well-known classes of quad nets, such as discrete minimal surfaces. The proposed construction does not rely on numerical optimization and allows control over axis positions and snap angles.

four-bar linkage , bistable structure , Study quadric , quadric net , infinitesimal flexibility , discrete surface , discrete Koenigs net

Introduction
Bistable or multistable structures, sometimes also called “snapping”, are an important topic of theoretical and applied research for at least three decades, often with a distinct interdisciplinary flavor that connects mechanism science, robotics, material science, bionics, physics, mathematics, computer science, etc. We do not even attempt to provide a full literature overview here but instead refer to the recent survey paper
[
41
]
. There, bistable structures are defined by the existence of “two distinct stable states” that require a “minimal energy input” to effect a “snap-through from the ‘natural’ (initial) state to the ‘everted’ state”.
In this text we propose a novel bistable structure, composed of four-bars that are connected according to the combinatorics of a quad net with revolute joints sitting on edges. We call these structures
snapping four-bar nets
. In contrast to many state-of-the-art they are truly spatial, exhibit no obvious symmetries and allow for clear geometric constructions that do not require optimization. While four-bar mechanisms (at least flexible ones) and structures composed from them are classical objects of study in mechanism science
[
10
,
11
,
19
]
or
[
40
, Chapter 5]
, these novel structures do not fit exactly into the classification given in
[
41
, Section 2]
which names structures based on bars and beams, curved shells, folding techniques based on origami, and composite materials. Our snapping four-bar nets are reminiscent of some origami structures and also related to “bistable scissor structures”
[
2
,
1
]
which are subsumed in the category of non-traditional designs in
[
41
]
. Both, origami and scissor structures can be modeled as linkages in the sense of mechanism science: They are composed of rigid bodies and revolute axes that can be grouped into
spherical
or
planar
linkages. In contrast, the snapping four-bar nets we propose allow for truly spatial axes arrangements that, when reduced to stripes in the quad net, produce the typical combinatorics of scissor linkages. In the flexible case, these have been used for the construction of Kempe linkages to planar and spatial rational curves
[
11
,
19
]
.
The basic building blocks for snapping four-bar nets are
snapping four-bar mechanisms.
These have been introduced in
[
39
]
and received some recent attention, either as simple examples and test cases for numeric “snappability” questions
[
23
,
24
,
42
]
or – in a rather specific situation – because of their relevance to Henrici’s flexible hyperboloid model
[
34
]
. We review some of their properties in Section
2
, address known construction methods, and explain the difficulties to apply them to larger structures.
Our main theoretical contribution is existence of snapping four-bar nets, based on a kinematic interpretation of snapping four-bars: The two configurations can be thought of as fixed and as moving axode of a “discrete Ribaucour motion”, a natural discretization of smooth Ribaucour motions that are characterized by having zero instantaneous pitch
[
32
]
. In this way, snapping four-bars are related to rotation quadrilaterals
[
30
,
31
]
and snapping four-bar nets appear as quad nets in the Study quadric
[
33
, Section 11]
. The existence of these quadric nets is guaranteed for net dimension
d
≤
6
d\leq 6
and also ensures existence of corresponding snapping four-bar nets. This we describe in Section
3
.
While this resolves theoretical issues related to the existence of snapping four-bar nets, practical examples are still difficult to realize as there is but little control over the position of individual revolute axes and over snap angles. We address this in Section
4
for the case

37
]
to obtain a pair of discrete quad surfaces with congruent corresponding faces. It gives rise to a discrete Ribaucour motion of dimension two whose discrete moving and fixed axodes produce the two configurations of a snapping four-bar net with a
surface-like appearance.
This approach is quite simple and straightforward but may seem rather particular. We prove that this is not the case and, in fact, a large class of snapping four-bar nets can be obtained in that way.
The constructions suggested in Section

to edges), in Section

To conclude this introduction, let us emphasize two important aspects that
distinguish snapping four-bar nets from most bistable structures known in
literature:
1.
Snapping four-bar nets allow for
exactly two configurations
in a
precise mathematical sense.
Their calculation requires only simple
geometric constructions or algebraic computations. No energy optimization is
involved but the construction leaves plenty degrees of freedom to optimize for
secondary goals.
2.
As a consequence of this exact geometric/algebraic construction, no energy
is stored in either of the two configurations of the snapping four-bar net. A
snap will only occur if energy is induced into the system. This might be
unusual in contemporary research on bistable structures but we view it is a
neutral property that may be advantageous or not, depending on the particular
application.

4
Surface Design
Having established existence of snapping four-bar nets, we now turn to their actual construction. In order to obtain feasible mechanisms, it is necessary to control the axes’ positions as well as the
snapping angles
, that is, the revolute angles in each axis between the two configurations. The former is important because the axes need to be connected by rigid links of reasonable size and shape, the latter is important for two reasons:
1.
Large snapping angles tend to come along with much strain on the links and
an increased risk of self collision during the snap.
2.
Small snapping angles will produce structures that are close to being shaky with an entirely different mechanical behavior.

Infinitesimally flexible frameworks which are combinatorially equivalent to the skeleton of the octahedron were characterized by Blaschke
[
4

[
20

(see also
[
18

[
38
]

These are polyhedral linkages where the links are the planar faces of a combinatorial octahedron whose edges serve as revolute axes. Because of the duality between hexahedron (cube) and octahedron, their linkgraph is, indeed, the
1
1
-skeleton of a cube. However, in these snapping octahedra, the three axes attached to one link are coplanar and any four axes attached to one face of the linkgraph are concurrent. This is not the case in our construction, at least not generically.
Wunderlich correctly mentions that flexible octahedra are even more remarkable than snapping ones. The same is true for the more general class of flexible linkages with cubes as linkgraph and (generically) non-intersecting axes. These have actually been presented in
[
12
]
but, yet again, feasible examples and visualizations are rare. Typically, they are either planar (as in
[
12
, Figure 2]
), or just closed-loop 6R sub-linkages, obtained from cyclic paths of length six on the linkgraph (as in
[
13
]
), or Bricard octahedra (with intersecting axes).

the linkgraph of a snapping cube. Hence, snapping closed-loop 6R linkages appear as sub-structures. Numerical examples of such objects have been presented in

). The bearings used in that model had an outer diameter of
12

and a height of
4

. To avoid geometric interference due to overlapping, some housings were shifted along their axes, which is always mechanically possible and does not affect the kinematic snapping behavior of the overall structure. This changes the original quadrangles of the underlying discrete surface but does not affect bistability.
In the second step, we designed the rigid links. In our initial attempt, we used pipe surfaces between adjacent joints, as shown in
[
16
]
. However, the resulting snapping behavior was not as well defined as expected. We therefore adopted the more robust design presented here: spline curves connecting the joints served as edge curves for NURBS surfaces, which were then offset to assign material thickness. During assembly, screws matching the inner diameter of the bearings were inserted as axes, a washer was positioned between the two inner bearings, and the stack was fastened with a nut.
CAD data and videos of the snapping structures presented in Figure
10

35

5
Conclusion
We presented a mathematical treatment of snapping four-bar nets, a mechanical
structure that is bistable in an exact mathematical sense. By identifying snapping four-bar nets with discrete rolling motions and with quad nets in the Study quadric, we proved existence of arbitrarily large structures. Moreover, we suggested a construction method, based on infinitesimally flexible quad surfaces and Whiteley de-averaging, that allows to control axis positions as well as snap angles. We summarize the essential construction steps here in order to help readers who wish to construct their own models:
1.
Pick a discrete quad net
f

with a face or vertex rigid infinitesimal isometric deformation. Many of those can be found in existing literature
[
7
,
26
,
36
]
.
2.
Apply Whiteley de-averaging to obtain a pair

+
f^{+}
,
f
−
f^{-}
of isometric quad nets with congruent faces or with congruent vertex stars.
3.
In case of congruent vertex stars, change the net combinatorics as illustrated in Figure
8
.
4.
Color the two surfaces in a checkerboard pattern and create a discrete rolling motion as illustrated in Figure
4
. The revolute axes in the fixed and in the moving frame, respectively, determine the two configurations of the snapping four-bar net.
5.
In case the
f

being a Koenigs net, use Equations (
9
) to control the snap angle and (
10
) to determine the axis directions directly.
3D printed prototypes verify the feasibility of the thus obtained surface-like designs. Of course, actual mechanism synthesis, with clear constraints imposed on both configurations, is still a challenge.
Further open questions concern problems that go beyond the mere geometry of snapping four-bar nets: Which deformations occur during the snap? Can they be modelled in such a way that collisions of links can be detected and avoided? Which force is needed to induce the snap and will the snap propagate through the net? Which tension is induced into the links during the snap. How should they be shaped and how can they be fabricated in order to ensure both, stiff target configurations and a long life-time?

preview_text:
We study mechanical structures composed of spatial four-bar linkages that are bistable, that is, they allow for two distinct configurations. They have an interpretation as quad nets in the Study quadric which can be used to prove existence of arbitrarily large structures of this type. We propose a purely geometric construction of such examples, starting from infinitesimally flexible quad nets in Euclidean space and applying Whiteley de-averaging. This point of view situates the problem within the broader framework of discrete differential geometry and enables the construction of bistable structures from well-known classes of quad nets, such as discrete minimal surfaces. The proposed construction does not rely on numerical optimization and allows control over axis positions and snap angles.
